name: Basalt Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Basalt. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Basalt surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-infrared wavelength optimized for Basalt absorption characteristics

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for effective cleaning resolution

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for continuous cleaning coverage

energyDensity.description: Fluence level for effective contaminant removal without substrate damage

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient ablation of surface contaminants

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform surface treatment

passCount.description: Number of cleaning passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning coverage

power.description: Typical range for industrial cleaning of rocks like basalt to remove contaminants without excessive heating; starting at 100-200 W to avoid thermal damage. Max limited by basalt's melting point (~1200-1400°C) and low thermal conductivity. Source: Manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics and Coherent for 1064 nm fiber lasers in stone cleaning applications; academic paper by S. J. H. Lee et al. (Opt. Lasers Eng., 2016) on rock surface preparation.

scan_speed.description: Slower speeds (100-500 mm/s) for heavy contamination like rust or coatings on basalt, faster (1000-2000 mm/s) for light dirt; optimal balances cleaning efficiency and heat accumulation. Source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Europe guidelines and equipment manuals (e.g., Adapt Laser Systems); case studies in J. Pou et al. (Appl. Surf. Sci., 2011) for stone ablation.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime (5-100 ns) preferred for basalt to achieve ablation with minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond reduces HAZ further but increases cost. Recommended ns for industrial scalability and basalt's properties. Source: Laser physics research in A. Nevosad et al. (J. Appl. Phys., 2008) on pulsed laser cleaning of geological materials; avoids thermal cracking in low-conductivity basalt.

repetition_rate.description: Typical 20-80 kHz for effective contaminant removal; higher rates speed up cleaning but risk heat buildup on basalt's poor thermal dissipation. Trade-off favors moderate rates for uniform results. Source: Academic literature including R. N. Raman et al. (Opt. Express, 2010) on repetition rate effects in stone cleaning; manufacturer data from Trumpf lasers.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for safe ablation of contaminants (e.g., oxides) on basalt; damage threshold ~4-5 J/cm² to prevent substrate cracking or melting. Conservative for basalt's high absorption at 1064 nm. Source: Ablation threshold studies in M. Autric et al. (Proc. SPIE, 1998) on rock materials; confirmed in industrial reports for 1064 nm cleaning.

spot_size.description: Smaller spots (100-200 μm) for precise surface preparation on basalt, larger (300-500 μm) for broad area cleaning; recommended balances resolution and efficiency for industrial use. Source: Equipment specifications from Raycus and SPI Lasers; optimization in F. Léonard et al. (Opt. Lasers Eng., 2015) for heterogeneous surfaces like basalt.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, 2-3 for heavy layers on basalt to ensure complete removal without over-ablation; multi-pass minimizes damage risk. Source: Industry practice from case studies in heritage stone cleaning (adaptable to industrial), e.g., EN 17121 standard and reports by the European Laser Cleaning Association; practical trials in basalt quarrying applications.

overlap_ratio.description: 30-50% for large areas to speed processing, 60-80% for uniform coverage and contaminant removal on textured basalt surfaces; prevents gaps in cleaning. Source: Process optimization studies in P. Pouli et al. (Appl. Phys. A, 2010) on scan strategies for stones; laser system manuals emphasizing overlap for even fluence distribution.
